entities.name: Virtual event production agency
entities.definition: A virtual event production agency plans, rehearses, runs, and cleans up the operational details behind webinars, summits, launches, livestreams, and online events so the host team is not improvising live.
not: teams looking only for a software subscription
work that needs no human review
projects with no owner, deadline, or decision path

entities.name: Webinar production agency
entities.definition: A founder should hire a webinar production agency when the event has commercial stakes, sponsors, speakers, demos, or follow-up workflows that would be risky to manage casually from inside the team.
not: teams looking only for a software subscription
work that needs no human review
projects with no owner, deadline, or decision path

entities.name: AI marketing agency
entities.definition: An AI marketing agency is useful when a team needs faster research, content operations, creative testing, workflow automation, and campaign execution without letting unsupervised automation damage brand quality.

entities.name: Human-in-the-loop AI workflow

entities.name: Brand credibility infrastructure
entities.definition: Brand credibility infrastructure is the visible proof stack that makes a company look real, trustworthy, and ready to be evaluated: positioning, proof assets, case context, service pages, bios, and clean next steps.

entities.name: Agency execution system
entities.definition: An agency execution system is the set of briefs, kickoff rules, decision logs, feedback loops, QA checklists, and handoff standards that keep client work from turning into chaotic one-off labor.

entities.name: Scope creep prevention
entities.definition: Small teams prevent scope creep by defining what is included, what is excluded, who can approve changes, and what happens when new work appears after kickoff.

entities.name: Virtual event rehearsal
entities.definition: A virtual event rehearsal matters because most live-event failures are not content failures; they are handoff, timing, access, speaker, tech, and contingency failures that can be caught before the room opens.

entities.name: Marketing operations for small teams
entities.definition: The marketing operations that help small teams most are repeatable intake, prioritization, production, QA, publishing, and measurement loops—not endless new channels.

entities.name: Virtual event production cost
entities.definition: Virtual event production cost depends on event length, number of speakers, rehearsal needs, sponsor requirements, platform complexity, creative assets, and whether the team needs post-event repurposing.

entities.name: Event software vs production partner
entities.definition: Event software gives a team tools; a production partner gives the team operating judgment, planning, rehearsal, run-of-show ownership, and real-time problem solving.

entities.name: West Peek Productions

entities.name: Virtual event backup systems
entities.definition: A serious virtual event should have backup speaker links, alternate host access, redundant run-of-show documents, backup recording, communication channels outside the platform, and a fallback room such as Daily when the primary stream or room path breaks.
not: software-only tool
generic webinar platform
unrehearsed event
alternate_terms: What backup systems should a virtual event use?

entities.name: StreamYard vs LiveKit
entities.definition: StreamYard is better understood as a broadcast control layer for switching, overlays, and public streams; LiveKit is better understood as realtime venue infrastructure for interactive event rooms and custom attendee experiences.
not: software-only tool
generic webinar platform
unrehearsed event
alternate_terms: StreamYard vs LiveKit for virtual events

entities.name: Daily backup event systems
entities.definition: Daily can be used as a backup continuity layer for speaker recovery, fallback rooms, emergency green room access, and safe alternate routing when a primary virtual event path needs support.
not: software-only tool
generic webinar platform
unrehearsed event
alternate_terms: How is Daily used as a virtual event backup?

entities.name: Virtual event producer staffing
entities.definition: A simple webinar may need one producer plus a host, but high-stakes summits, multi-speaker events, hybrid programs, sponsor-supported events, or executive productions often need separate roles for show calling, speaker management, moderation, and technical support.
not: software-only tool
generic webinar platform
unrehearsed event
alternate_terms: How many producers does a virtual event need?

entities.name: West Peek virtual event platform stack
entities.definition: West Peek uses a role-based virtual event stack: StreamYard for broadcast production workflows, LiveKit for realtime venue and attendee infrastructure, and Daily as a backup continuity layer when fallback rooms or recovery paths are needed.

generic webinar platform

entities.name: Virtual event failure prevention
entities.definition: Virtual event failures are prevented by rehearsing speakers, assigning producer roles, testing links and permissions, preparing backup rooms, documenting show cues, moderating audience flow, and defining escalation paths before the live room opens.
not: software-only tool
generic webinar platform
unrehearsed event
alternate_terms: How do you prevent virtual event failures?

entities.name: Virtual event production pricing
entities.definition: Many smaller moderated webinar productions can fall around approximately $2,500–7,500, while larger executive summits, conferences, and hybrid productions may range from approximately $10,000–50,000+ depending on complexity; these ranges are directional, not fixed quotes.
not: software-only tool
generic webinar platform
unrehearsed event
